problem: Recursive neural networks need parse trees; learning discrete, task-specific tree structures from plain text is hard because composition choices are non-differentiable and prior soft/RL approaches are slow or ambiguous.
method: Gumbel Tree-LSTM that builds trees bottom-up: forms Tree-LSTM parent candidates from adjacent nodes, scores them with a learned composition query vector, and selects a parent via Straight-Through Gumbel-Softmax (argmax at test), enabling end-to-end backprop through discrete choices; the layer-wise, easy-first selection supports batching and yields fast convergence.
paper_title: Learning to Compose Task-Specific Tree Structures

problem: Choosing compositions using only local word pairs lacks global sentence context without a shift-reduce tracker.
method: LSTM-based leaf transformation that runs a sequence LSTM over the input to produce contextualized leaf hidden/cell states before recursive composition, combining sequential context with latent tree building to improve accuracy and convergence.
paper_title: Learning to Compose Task-Specific Tree Structures

problem: CBMA tools like Neurosynth yield reliable brain maps only for single-term queries and cannot express or soundly evaluate complex logic-based (conjunctive/disjunctive) queries over term–study associations.
method: Design a probabilistic domain-specific language grounded in Datalog and CP-Logic that encodes CBMA databases; translate the program to an equivalent Bayesian network to prove that answers compute the correct voxel-activation posteriors for complex term queries.
paper_title: Complex Coordinate-Based Meta-Analysis with Probabilistic Programming

problem: Knowledge compilation approaches (e.g., SDDs in ProbLog) are too slow to evaluate multi-term queries at whole-brain scale with thousands of terms, studies, and voxels.
method: Impose non-recursive, tuple-independent syntactic restrictions and apply lifted query processing for unions of conjunctive queries (via the Dalvi–Suciu dichotomy), generating extensional query plans that run in polynomial time and scale to large neuroimaging datasets.
paper_title: Complex Coordinate-Based Meta-Analysis with Probabilistic Programming

problem: Hard TF-IDF thresholding to link terms and studies excludes many relevant studies, causing severe loss of power in multi-term conjunctive CBMA queries.
method: Replace hard thresholds with a logistic soft-threshold ω(x; α, τ) to model term–study association probabilities within the probabilistic program, enabling closed-form estimates for conjunctions/disjunctions and improving power and cross-subsample consistency on two-term queries in simulations and Neurosynth.
paper_title: Complex Coordinate-Based Meta-Analysis with Probabilistic Programming

problem: Mental-health LLMs blend multiple counseling styles and ignore that each counselor has a distinct linguistic style and therapeutic technique; collecting large volumes of counselor-specific sessions to build a digital twin is costly and slow.
method: PsyDT introduces a dynamic one-shot synthesis pipeline: from a handful of verified real sessions per topic, GPT-4 summarizes a target counselor’s linguistic style and identifies therapy type; a therapy-knowledge base is retrieved per type (e.g., REBT); GPT-4 simulates client Big Five traits from single-turn questions to diversify client behavior; then GPT-4 expands single-turn questions into topic-matched multi-turn dialogues conditioned on (style + therapy + personality), yielding PsyDTCorpus quickly and cost-effectively.
paper_title: PsyDT: Using LLMs to Construct the Digital Twin of Psychological Counselor with Personalized Counseling Style for Psychological Counseling

problem: Even with synthetic data, it is challenging to deploy an LLM that reliably behaves like a specific counselor across multi-turn interactions while maintaining empathy, safety, and technique fidelity.
method: Multi-turn instruction fine-tuning (MIFT) of a base LLM (e.g., Qwen2-7B-Instruct) on PsyDTCorpus to produce PsyDTLLM, a counselor-specific digital twin. The model preserves the counselor’s linguistic style and therapy application while improving conversational strategy and empathy, verified via LLM-based style/technique similarity scoring, expert evaluations, and ablations showing the necessity of style, therapy, and personality conditioning.
paper_title: PsyDT: Using LLMs to Construct the Digital Twin of Psychological Counselor with Personalized Counseling Style for Psychological Counseling

problem: LLM self-refinement depends on the model evaluating its own outputs, but there is no formal way to quantify whether it systematically favors its own generations (self-bias), leading to unreliable self-correction.
method: Formally define self-bias with two complementary statistics: (1) score inflation (statistical bias of model-estimated quality vs. true/human-aligned quality) and (2) distance skewness to capture asymmetry of over/under-estimation. Instantiate task-appropriate ground truths (BLEURT quantile-mapped to MQM for MT, strict concept coverage for constrained generation, exact correctness for math) and measure bias across six LLMs, four languages, and three tasks.
paper_title: Pride and Prejudice: LLM Amplifies Self-Bias in Self-Refinement

problem: Self-refine and self-rewarding pipelines often degrade true quality because iterative procedures may amplify biased self-evaluations, optimizing for false positives and stylistic self-preference rather than real improvements.
method: Comprehensive empirical analysis showing universal and iterative amplification of self-bias across MT, constrained generation, and math; demonstrate divergence between self-estimated and true gains, preference for own style via paraphrasing experiments, and bias amplification with larger candidate pools in self-rewarding (k-sampling) settings.
paper_title: Pride and Prejudice: LLM Amplifies Self-Bias in Self-Refinement

problem: How to mitigate self-bias so that self-refinement yields genuine performance gains instead of inflated self-assessments.
method: Propose and validate two mitigations: (i) replace/self-augment self-feedback with an external low-bias evaluator (InstructScore) that provides fine-grained, reference-based error annotations, leading to lower bias and actual BLEURT gains during refinement; (ii) scale model size (e.g., LLaMA2 70B vs. 7B/13B), which reduces bias growth and improves robustness across iterations.
paper_title: Pride and Prejudice: LLM Amplifies Self-Bias in Self-Refinement

problem: LLM-based planners offer strong reasoning and interpretability but are too slow and resource-intensive for real-time closed-loop autonomous driving.
method: AsyncDriver decouples LLM inference from the real-time planner via asynchronous control: the LLM runs sparsely to produce persistent high-level, scene-associated instruction features that guide multiple planner steps, reducing latency (~40% speedup at 3-frame intervals) with minimal performance loss.
paper_title: Asynchronous Large Language Model Enhanced Planner for Autonomous Driving

problem: Real-time planners struggle to understand and follow complex, language-based routing instructions while operating on vectorized scene data, limiting controllability.
method: A Scene-Associated Instruction Feature Extraction Module uses Llama2-13B to process vectorized map/agent embeddings plus a sequence of routing instructions, and an Adaptive Injection Block injects these features into any transformer-based planner via cross-attention and a learnable adaptive gate, improving controllability and prediction accuracy.
paper_title: Asynchronous Large Language Model Enhanced Planner for Autonomous Driving

problem: Misalignment between linguistic instructions and vectorized scene features hinders effective guidance of the planner.
method: An Alignment Assistance Module adds training-only auxiliary tasks (ego kinematics regression, adjacent-lane presence, traffic light state, lane-change need, future velocity decision) to align multimodal representations, yielding better closed-loop performance without runtime cost.
paper_title: Asynchronous Large Language Model Enhanced Planner for Autonomous Driving

problem: Existing long-context benchmarks are too short for modern LLM windows, use outdated documents causing data leakage, and mostly test short-range dependencies rather than true long-range reasoning.
method: Introduce LooGLE, a long-context, generic evaluation benchmark built from post-2022 arXiv/Wikipedia/movie-TV scripts with extra-long documents (~19k words avg) and 6,448 instances, including 1,101 manually crafted, cross-validated long-dependency QA pairs spanning multiple categories (multi-evidence retrieval, timeline reorder, computation, comprehension), alongside short-dependency QA/cloze; open-sourced pipeline for periodic refresh.
paper_title: LooGLE: Can Long-Context Language Models Understand Long Contexts?

problem: Timeline reordering over long documents is hard to score reliably due to heterogeneous output formats and the need to compare event sequences robustly.
method: Standardize timeline evaluation by enforcing Roman-numeral outputs and introducing four sequence-deviation metrics (LSD, LMD, SD, SDD) with preprocessing to detect non-standard outputs, yielding format-robust automatic scoring.
paper_title: LooGLE: Can Long-Context Language Models Understand Long Contexts?

problem: It is unclear which strategies (longer context windows, retrieval, CoT) actually improve LLM long-context understanding beyond simple tasks like summarization.
method: Run a systematic evaluation of 8 SOTA LLMs and retrieval-augmented pipelines on LooGLE with GPT-4-as-judge and ablations on input length, retrieval, and CoT; findings show commercial > open-source; models handle short tasks but struggle with long-dependency QA; enlarging context windows alone has limited benefit; retrieval helps short QA but not complex long-dependency; CoT gives modest gains mainly for timeline reorder and computation.
paper_title: LooGLE: Can Long-Context Language Models Understand Long Contexts?

problem: LLM-as-a-Judge is widely used to evaluate personalization but its reliability for inferring user preferences from personas is unvalidated and potentially low, especially when personas are simplistic or irrelevant (persona sparsity).
method: Conduct a systematic validation of LLM-as-a-Personalized-Judge across PRISM, OpinionQA, Empathetic Conversation, and Personal Reddit with multiple models (GPT-4, GPT-3.5, Command R+, Llama-3 70B), measuring agreement with first-person ground truth and analyzing tie options; identify persona sparsity as a key failure mode with substantially lower and inconsistent agreement than previously assumed.
paper_title: Can LLM be a Personalized Judge?

problem: Personalization judgments can be unreliable when persona information is insufficient; evaluators need a scalable way to avoid overconfident wrong calls and focus on trustworthy samples.
method: Augment the judge with verbal uncertainty estimation—eliciting a 1–100 self-reported certainty score per judgment and applying confidence thresholding—to filter uncertain cases. This certainty-aware pipeline yields >80% agreement on high-certainty subsets (binary tasks), shows well-calibrated confidence for stronger models, and uses confidence distribution as a proxy for task/sample difficulty; ablations varying persona features verify confidence appropriately drops with less-informative personas.
paper_title: Can LLM be a Personalized Judge?

problem: It is unclear whether an LLM-as-a-Personalized-Judge can match third-person human evaluators on preference inference from personas.
method: Run a controlled crowdsourcing study on OpinionQA where annotators infer preferences and report certainty; compare to GPT-4 under the same prompts with majority-vote aggregation and bootstrap analysis. Results show comparable overall accuracy to humans and superior accuracy on high-certainty samples, validating the approach as a scalable proxy when first-person data is unavailable.
paper_title: Can LLM be a Personalized Judge?

problem: Text-to-image diffusion models lack a unified way to accept arbitrary interleaved text and multiple image references; prior LMM–diffusion integrations rely on heavy heads, discrete tokens, or task-specific designs and struggle with compositional control.
method: DREAM ENGINE replaces SD3.5’s text encoders with a frozen LMM (Qwen2-VL) plus a lightweight 2-layer MLP adapter that maps LMM hidden states into the DiT conditioning space, removing token-length limits. A Stage-1 alignment trains only the adapter via (A) text→image alignment on captioned images and (B) self-supervised image→image alignment, leveraging the LMM’s unified representation to enable interleaved text–image conditioning with minimal parameter updates.
paper_title: Multimodal Representation Alignment for Image Generation: Text-Image Interleaved Control Is Easier Than You Think

problem: Generative models inadequately follow complex interleaved instructions that compose elements from multiple images or perform rich edits.
method: Stage-2 interleaved instruction tuning unfreezes the DiT (via LoRA) and trains on (C) free-form image editing (UltraEdit) and (D) a new object-driven generation task constructed from detection datasets by sampling multiple objects and pairing with captions. This yields strong instruction following and emergent cross-image feature mixing for compositional synthesis.
paper_title: Multimodal Representation Alignment for Image Generation: Text-Image Interleaved Control Is Easier Than You Think

problem: Editing and object-driven generation require controllable trade-offs between faithfulness to reference images and creative variation, which standard pipelines lack.
method: A visual feature blending mechanism adds a skip connection that combines ViT image features with LLM output token features for image patches using a tunable blending ratio r. Training with r sampled from [0,1] enables inference-time control over visual consistency versus diversity.
paper_title: Multimodal Representation Alignment for Image Generation: Text-Image Interleaved Control Is Easier Than You Think

problem: Social-media depression detection struggles to distinguish clinical depression from transient mood and lacks clinically grounded, longitudinal evidence.
method: DORIS uses an LLM to (i) annotate each post with DSM-5 symptoms (9-category) and (ii) synthesize a longitudinal mood-course summary from emotionally intensive posts. These are converted into structured, clinically aligned features: an averaged 9-D symptom vector and a mood-course representation that blends the LLM summary embedding with the mean embedding of filtered emotional posts (weighted by α, β).
paper_title: Depression Detection on Social Media with Large Language Models

problem: Direct LLM classification is unreliable/low-precision and traditional models lack interpretability, yet high accuracy and trustworthy outputs are required.
method: A hybrid pipeline trains Gradient Boosting Trees on interpretable features—diagnostic-criteria vector, mood-course representation, and overall post-history embedding—fusing by summing mood-course with history embeddings and concatenating the DSM-5 vector. Post-hoc probability calibration (e.g., isotonic regression) improves reliability. The system also uses the LLM to generate explanations grounded in the identified symptoms and mood course.
paper_title: Depression Detection on Social Media with Large Language Models

problem: LLM annotation of all posts is computationally costly and most posts are irrelevant or low-emotion, causing waste.
method: An efficient prefiltering scheme uses clinician-crafted and iteratively validated DSM-5 symptom templates and emotion templates. Posts and templates are embedded; only the top-k% high-similarity (high-risk) posts are LLM-annotated (others set to zero), and high-emotion posts are selected for mood-course construction. This preserves performance close to full annotation at a fraction of cost.
paper_title: Depression Detection on Social Media with Large Language Models

problem: Lack of large, diverse, real-world benchmarks to evaluate open-ended interleaved (image+text) generation, leading to poor coverage, contamination risks, and inability to assess multi-step coherence.
method: OpenING benchmark: 5,400 high-quality human-annotated, multi-step interleaved instances across 56 tasks and 23 meta-topics, curated via a standardized annotation/filtering pipeline, bilingual processing, and careful prompt design; split into Dev/Test to support judge training and model evaluation.
paper_title: OpenING: A Comprehensive Benchmark for Judging Open-ended Interleaved Image-Text Generation

problem: Open-ended multimodal evaluation is unreliable and costly with GPT-based judges due to bias, privacy/cost concerns, and low alignment with human preferences.
method: IntJudge: an offline judge model built on Qwen2-VL-7B, trained on pairwise comparisons with a Reference-Augmented Generation (RAG) pipeline and multi-objective loss (CE/contrastive/MSE/pairwise ranking), achieving 82.42% agreement with human judgments (+11.34% over GPT-4o).
paper_title: OpenING: A Comprehensive Benchmark for Judging Open-ended Interleaved Image-Text Generation

problem: Subjective scoring of interleaved outputs is unstable; fair large-scale comparison across many models/tasks is difficult.
method: Interleaved Arena: a pairwise, arena-style evaluation framework with seven explicit criteria and a roulette matching algorithm to sample battle pairs ensuring agent/task coverage; supports human, GPT, and IntJudge assessments with defined tie-handling strategies for robust, reproducible rankings.
paper_title: OpenING: A Comprehensive Benchmark for Judging Open-ended Interleaved Image-Text Generation

problem: There is no benchmark that holistically tests LLM agents' rule learning in interactive settings with fine-grained actions and truly unknown rules; existing QA or coarse environments do not require abduction–deduction–induction cycles.
method: Introduce RULEARN, a text-based interactive benchmark of 300 manually designed puzzles across Function Operator, Escape Room, and Reactor environments, featuring fine-grained action spaces (arbitrary string inputs with character-level feedback) and hidden, previously unseen rules expressed/learned in natural language.
paper_title: IDEA: Enhancing the Rule Learning Ability of Large Language Model Agent through Induction, Deduction, and Abduction

problem: LLM agents struggle to discover and apply rules in unfamiliar interactive environments, often taking uninformed, repetitive actions due to the lack of an integrated reasoning process.
method: Propose IDEA, an agent framework that iteratively integrates abduction (generate initial hypothesis from sparse observations), deduction (devise and execute plans to test/use the hypothesis), and induction (refine the hypothesis from feedback), with explicit memory, hypothesis, and plan components and tailored prompting for each action; yields ~10% higher success and ~30% fewer repeated actions vs. a ReAct baseline across multiple LLMs.
paper_title: IDEA: Enhancing the Rule Learning Ability of Large Language Model Agent through Induction, Deduction, and Abduction

problem: It is unclear how LLM rule-learning behavior differs from humans across reasoning stages, hindering targeted improvements.
method: Conduct a fine-grained human-vs-LLM study on RULEARN with stage-wise evaluations (abduction correctness, deduction plan effectiveness, induction effectiveness and sample efficiency), revealing LLMs' overconfident abduction, weaker planning, and poor hypothesis refinement compared to humans.
paper_title: IDEA: Enhancing the Rule Learning Ability of Large Language Model Agent through Induction, Deduction, and Abduction

problem: Generative NER trained with entity-centric schemas ignores non-entity text, leading to weak zero-shot generalization, noisy type confusions, and fuzzy entity boundaries.
method: GNER: a token-by-token generative NER schema that explicitly incorporates negative instances (non-entity 'O' tokens) and context, combined with BIO tagging to sharpen boundaries; instruction tuning with class order shuffling and external entity sampling improves robustness, yielding higher precision/recall and fewer unlabeled/noisy/boundary errors.
paper_title: Rethinking Negative Instances for Generative Named Entity Recognition

problem: Unstructured generative outputs often contain omissions/additions/substitutions, making span recovery brittle and naive LCS-based alignment slow (O(N^2)).
method: An optimized LCS Matching algorithm to map generated tokens back to the input with O(N log N) complexity and back tokenization–based matching conditions; it robustly aligns noisy generations and converts them into structured entities, improving accuracy and achieving large speedups on long sequences.
paper_title: Rethinking Negative Instances for Generative Named Entity Recognition

problem: Beam search typically harms entity-centric generative NER because early mistakes persist and cannot leverage later context.
method: Under the token-by-token, context-aware schema with negative instances, apply beam search to enable a self-correction mechanism where later tokens revise earlier labels, consistently improving zero-shot F1 as beam size increases.
paper_title: Rethinking Negative Instances for Generative Named Entity Recognition

problem: Unsupervised reconstruction-based time series anomaly detectors can overfit and reconstruct unlabeled anomalies in training data, making it hard to capture normal temporal patterns and distinguish discontinuous anomalies.
method: Model time series as a continuous implicit neural representation (INR) and use a transformer hypernetwork to predict INR parameters in a single forward pass. Leverage INR’s spectral bias to prioritize low-frequency (smooth, normal) components so high-frequency, discontinuous anomalies reconstruct poorly and are exposed by reconstruction error.
paper_title: TSINR: Capturing Temporal Continuity via Implicit Neural Representations for Time Series Anomaly Detection

problem: Capturing diverse temporal structures (trend, seasonality, complex residuals) and both inter- and intra-channel dependencies in multivariate time series is difficult for standard reconstruction models.
method: Design a specialized INR decomposition with three components: low-degree polynomial (trend), Fourier series (seasonal), and a residual block with a group-based architecture—global layers for inter-channel coupling and group layers for per-channel detail—enhancing representation and anomaly separability.
paper_title: TSINR: Capturing Temporal Continuity via Implicit Neural Representations for Time Series Anomaly Detection

problem: Whether machine-translated instruction data is sufficient for multilingual instruction tuning, and whether translated benchmarks faithfully reveal gaps versus native data.
method: Conduct controlled monolingual instruction tuning in Spanish, Russian, and Chinese on eight base LLMs (Llama 2, Gemma, Qwen) using either native Aya data or equal-volume translations (Google Translate, Cohere), and evaluate on nine benchmarks spanning native vs translated and structured vs generative tasks (TyDi QA, CMMLU, C-Eval, XQuAD, MGSM, MT-/HT-MMLU, open-ended QA with LLM judges). Findings: native-tuned models consistently outperform on native and generative tests; translated structured tests (e.g., MGSM, MT/HT-MMLU) often mask differences; the native–translated gap grows with model strength and is most pronounced on generative tasks.
paper_title: Is It Good Data for Multilingual Instruction Tuning or Just Bad Multilingual Evaluation for Large Language Models?

problem: Identify whether performance gaps arise from translation artifacts or from missing language-specific knowledge in translated instruction data.
method: Create round-trip translated (RTT) instruction sets by translating native data X→English→X to preserve native-origin knowledge while injecting translationese, and compare models trained on RTT vs single-pass English-origin translations on native benchmarks (TyDi QA-ru, CMMLU-zh). Result: RTT generally outperforms English-origin translations, indicating that missing native knowledge is more detrimental than translation defects.
paper_title: Is It Good Data for Multilingual Instruction Tuning or Just Bad Multilingual Evaluation for Large Language Models?

problem: How to mitigate performance gaps when only translated instruction data is available.
method: Apply regularization during instruction tuning via a lower learning rate (1e-6) and multilingual instruction tuning with downsampled data across eight languages; evaluate across native and translated, structured and generative tests. Outcome: these strategies can close the native–translated gap on structured tasks (e.g., TyDi QA, CMMLU) for some base models, but they fail to bridge the gap on generative tasks (e.g., XQuAD, open-ended QA).
paper_title: Is It Good Data for Multilingual Instruction Tuning or Just Bad Multilingual Evaluation for Large Language Models?

problem: Subtle anomalies may not stand out in raw space, limiting sensitivity of reconstruction errors, especially across channels.
method: Introduce a frozen pre-trained LLM encoder to map raw time series into a feature domain that amplifies anomaly fluctuations over time and aligns anomaly intervals across channels, improving INR sensitivity to abnormal regions without extra training at inference.
paper_title: TSINR: Capturing Temporal Continuity via Implicit Neural Representations for Time Series Anomaly Detection

problem: LLMs struggle with stepwise mathematical correctness; sentence-level SFT/DPO ignores process fidelity, while RL is costly and unstable.
method: Introduce SPPD, a process-level preference optimization derived from a step-level MDP and Bellman optimality, yielding a step-DPO loss with a dynamic value margin V*(s_w)-V*(s_l). Use a process-based Bradley–Terry model and approximate V* with a PRM; apply reward equivalence to scale margins via a controllable γ.
paper_title: SPPD: Self-training with Process Preference Learning Using Dynamic Value Margin

problem: Creating high-quality step-level preference data typically requires stronger model distillation or human labels, and token-level decoding cannot ensure comparable steps with shared context.
method: Design tree-based self-sampling on the model’s own logits to build prefix trees that yield multiple trajectories with common prefixes. Score each step using a PRM and select preference pairs by large score gaps, enabling fully self-generated, distillation-free step preferences.
paper_title: SPPD: Self-training with Process Preference Learning Using Dynamic Value Margin

problem: Preference learning methods lack a clear connection to online RL, and PPO/GRPO incur high compute and instability.
method: Prove that optimizing the offline every-step preference loss (with the proposed rewards) is equivalent to the on-policy policy gradient of a preference decoding model, justifying stable, RL-free self-training; further smooth training via PRM-filtered SFT and sentence-level DPO warm-up.
paper_title: SPPD: Self-training with Process Preference Learning Using Dynamic Value Margin

problem: Existing visual commonsense benchmarks are template-based, text-biased, and do not fairly evaluate both text-only LLMs and vision-augmented LMs (VaLMs).
method: Introduce IMAGENETVC, a human-annotated benchmark of 4,076 natural-language QA pairs spanning 1,000 ImageNet categories and five attribute types (color, shape, material, component, others), constructed via a multi-stage crowd pipeline using many images per category and cross-checks; includes region- and object-based questions and supports zero-/few-shot evaluation with visual grounding.
paper_title: IMAGENETVC: Zero- and Few-Shot Visual Commonsense Evaluation on 1000 ImageNet Categories

problem: It is unclear how well current LLMs and VaLMs possess visual commonsense and what factors (model scale, prompts, ICL) drive it.
method: Provide a standardized zero-/few-shot cloze-style evaluation across major LLM and VaLM families, with prompt calibration and in-context learning; conduct large-scale analyses revealing emergent visual commonsense around ~1.3B parameters (e.g., on components) and showing ICL both boosts accuracy and markedly improves calibration (e.g., r from 0.57 to 0.98) while reducing prompt variance.
paper_title: IMAGENETVC: Zero- and Few-Shot Visual Commonsense Evaluation on 1000 ImageNet Categories

problem: VaLM visual commonsense is undervalued without proper visual inputs and there is little guidance on effective image sources/quantity.
method: Establish a visual grounding protocol using web-retrieved and diffusion-synthesized images ranked by CLIP, and evaluate prefix-based (BLIP-2, MAGMA) and ensemble-based (Z-LaVI) VaLMs; show that CLIP-ranked relevant images significantly improve performance (especially color/material) and that gains saturate around top-10 images, and highlight the added benefits of ICL for VaLMs.
paper_title: IMAGENETVC: Zero- and Few-Shot Visual Commonsense Evaluation on 1000 ImageNet Categories

problem: Chinese medical Q&A datasets lack explicit instructions, limiting models' instruction-following and generalization.
method: Question-oriented instruction generation via chained examples: score seed examples with an LLM, sort from low to high quality, and build a progressive chain-of-thought prompt (E1 + instruct -> E2 + improved instruct* -> ... -> EN) to guide the LLM to produce higher-quality, question-specific instructions.
paper_title: DPF-CM: A Data Processing Framework with Privacy-Preserving Vector Databases for Chinese Medical LLMs Training and Deployment

problem: Preference datasets for RLHF/DPO contain contradictory, indistinguishable, or overly obvious pairs that inject noise and harm alignment.
method: Ensemble-based denoising: train multiple reward models and use the averaged preference distance r(x, y_c) - r(x, y_r) to identify and remove noisy pairs (consistently negative or excessively large distances) via thresholding.
paper_title: DPF-CM: A Data Processing Framework with Privacy-Preserving Vector Databases for Chinese Medical LLMs Training and Deployment

problem: Deployed medical LLMs risk leaking memorized training data, violating patient privacy.
method: PPVD (Privacy Preserving Vector Database): detect memorized samples via model-memory search (prompt/label split and ROUGE-L); build a high-risk embedding DB from intermediate model embeddings; construct a secure DB by pairing prompts with general-LLM responses; at inference, match intermediate embeddings to the high-risk DB and replace with secure DB content when similarity exceeds a threshold (match-and-replace).
paper_title: DPF-CM: A Data Processing Framework with Privacy-Preserving Vector Databases for Chinese Medical LLMs Training and Deployment

problem: Transformers’ quadratic self-attention limits long-context efficiency, while RNNs are hard to train in parallel and underperform at scale.
method: Introduce RWKV, a receptance-weighted key–value architecture with exact linear attention via per-channel exponential time-decay (WKV) and gating, enabling Transformer-like parallel training and RNN-like inference with O(Td) time and O(d) memory.
paper_title: RWKV: Reinventing RNNs for the Transformer Era

problem: Scaling dense RNNs to LLM sizes is hindered by training instability and inefficient kernels, preventing parity with Transformers.
method: Use time-parallel computation with a custom CUDA WKV kernel, time-dependent softmax for stability, token-shift mixing, output gating, and tailored initializations (small-init embeddings, near-identity inits) to stably train models up to 14B parameters with performance on par with similarly sized Transformers and matching scaling laws.
paper_title: RWKV: Reinventing RNNs for the Transformer Era

problem: Exploiting very long contexts efficiently in language modeling without quadratic costs.
method: Progressively finetune RWKV to longer sequence lengths (1024→8192) leveraging its recurrent state and learned per-channel time decay, achieving linear, constant-memory inference and improved test loss; demonstrate strong long-range performance on LRA tasks.
paper_title: RWKV: Reinventing RNNs for the Transformer Era

problem: Human evaluation of generated text is costly, hard to reproduce, and often unstable, impeding fair comparisons of NLP systems.
method: Introduce LLMevaluation: present LLMs with the exact instructions, samples, and Likert-scale questions used for human raters; let the model answer freely and parse scores from its output. Validate on two tasks—open-ended story generation and adversarial attacks—showing strong alignment with expert teachers: higher ratings for human-written vs GPT‑2 stories, meaningful Kendall’s τ correlations per attribute, correct ranking of benign vs adversarial samples and between attack methods, and capacity to provide rationales.
paper_title: Can Large Language Models Be an Alternative to Human Evaluation?

problem: LLM-based evaluations might be sensitive to prompt wording and sampling randomness, threatening reliability.
method: Conduct a robustness study by varying instruction formats (adding a human-worker persona; requesting explanations) and decoding temperature while keeping nucleus sampling fixed. Observe only small absolute score shifts (typically <0.25) that preserve system rankings; lower temperature increases agreement across samples, indicating stable, reproducible evaluations under reasonable prompt/generation changes.
paper_title: Can Large Language Models Be an Alternative to Human Evaluation?

problem: It is unclear which LLMs are suitable as surrogate evaluators and whether alignment training matters.
method: Compare T0, text-curie-001, text-davinci-003, and ChatGPT on the same evaluation protocols, measuring inter-annotator agreement and statistical significance. Find that strong RLHF-tuned models (text-davinci-003, ChatGPT) reliably mirror expert preferences and exhibit higher consistency, while T0/curie show weak or inconsistent preferences and low agreement—highlighting that model quality and alignment are critical for dependable LLMevaluation.
paper_title: Can Large Language Models Be an Alternative to Human Evaluation?

problem: Fragmented understanding of when and how to integrate non-text modalities into retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) for LLMs leads to ad‑hoc designs.
method: Define and consolidate multimodal RAG as a coherent paradigm; provide a taxonomy across modalities (image, code, structured knowledge, audio, video), retrieval vs. synthesis stages, and applications, highlighting connections and shared challenges (factuality, reasoning, interpretability, robustness).
paper_title: Retrieving Multimodal Information for Augmented Generation: A Survey

problem: Lack of modality-specific guidance on retrieval targets, indexing, and fusion strategies hampers effective grounded generation.
method: Systematically review 146 papers, organizing per modality the retrieval units (e.g., images/captions, code snippets/ASTs, KG subgraphs/tables, audio snippets, video moments), retriever/encoder designs (sparse/dense, multimodal encoders), and generation conditioning methods (prompting, constrained decoding, visual tokens), with application-wise exemplars (VQA, code gen/repair, table QA, audio captioning, video dialogue/captioning).
paper_title: Retrieving Multimodal Information for Augmented Generation: A Survey

problem: No clear roadmap for scaling and strengthening multimodal RAG in practice.
method: Propose future directions: (i) retrieval-augmented multimodal chain-of-thought reasoning, (ii) a unified multimodal knowledge index with compact dense representations and fast, dynamically updatable search (e.g., LSH), and (iii) retrieval-in-the-loop pretraining with tool/API calls to align models with external knowledge sources.
paper_title: Retrieving Multimodal Information for Augmented Generation: A Survey

problem: Robots in partially observable settings must choose epistemic actions across modalities and ground LLM reasoning in continuous control to resolve uncertainty.
method: Matcha agent: an LLM-centered "chat-with-the-environment" loop that autoregressively issues epistemic actions (knock, touch, weigh, pick) and reasons over multimodal feedback converted to natural language by modular perception units, planning until information sufficiency is reached.
paper_title: Chat with the Environment: Interactive Multimodal Perception Using Large Language Models

problem: Fusing heterogeneous sensory data numerically is brittle and hard to transfer; inferring latent object properties (e.g., material) requires integrating ambiguous multimodal cues.
method: Translate each modality’s output (ViLD vision, impact-sound classifier, haptics, weight) into templated natural-language descriptors (distinct or indistinct), enabling the LLM to perform semantic fusion and deduction, outperforming single-modality (sound-only) baselines.
paper_title: Chat with the Environment: Interactive Multimodal Perception Using Large Language Models

problem: LLMs often emit unexecutable actions for robots with constrained capabilities.
method: A few-shot, API-style prompt that enumerates valid robot.skill(object) commands with exemplars, paired with a language-conditioned control policy for execution; demonstrates that larger LLMs adhere to the action grammar and yield higher task success than smaller models.
paper_title: Chat with the Environment: Interactive Multimodal Perception Using Large Language Models

problem: VideoQA data is either costly to annotate or lacks fine-grained spatiotemporal detail; automatic methods using metadata or exhaustive frame-by-frame analysis produce shallow or redundant QA pairs.
method: VideoEspresso: an automatic pipeline that maps frames to language via InternVL2-8B captions, removes redundancy using BGE-M3 semantic similarity with LIFO filtering, groups frames to preserve temporal coherence, and uses GPT-4o to generate and LLMs to filter QA pairs. It further adds multimodal Chain-of-Thought by selecting core frames, extracting key objects and textual evidence, grounding spatially with GroundingDINO bounding boxes and temporally via BGE-M3 retrieval, yielding >200K fine-grained, spatiotemporally grounded QA-CoT annotations.
paper_title: VideoEspresso: A Large-Scale Chain-of-Thought Dataset for Fine-Grained Video Reasoning via Core Frame Selection

problem: LVLMs for video reasoning are inefficient and error-prone due to redundant frames and lack of explicit reasoning guidance.
method: Hybrid LVLMs Collaboration: a lightweight selector (1B LVLM captioner + 0.5/1.5B LLM) picks question-driven core frames, followed by a two-stage instruction-finetuned reasoning LVLM that first generates multimodal evidence then answers via CoT. This plug-and-play selector sharply reduces frames/FLOPs while the evidence-first training improves accuracy and transparency.
paper_title: VideoEspresso: A Large-Scale Chain-of-Thought Dataset for Fine-Grained Video Reasoning via Core Frame Selection

problem: Open-ended video reasoning lacks a robust, scalable evaluation covering diverse reasoning skills.
method: A 14-task benchmark with a two-step semantic evaluation (semantic similarity thresholding then distractor-based disambiguation) plus GPT-4o-driven subjective scoring (logic, factuality, accuracy, conciseness), enabling reliable assessment of complex video reasoning and demonstrating superior performance of the proposed method over 9 LVLM baselines.
paper_title: VideoEspresso: A Large-Scale Chain-of-Thought Dataset for Fine-Grained Video Reasoning via Core Frame Selection

problem: End-to-end language-guided robot planners lack interpretability and logical guarantees, while symbolic planners require machine-readable PDDL problem descriptions that are tedious to author from natural language and raw scene observations.
method: ViLaIn: a modular vision-language interpreter that generates full PDDL problem descriptions (objects, initial state, goals) from an instruction and an image. It combines an open-vocabulary detector (Grounding DINO) with domain-knowledge-augmented text queries for object lists, BLIP-2 per-object captioning plus GPT-4 few-shot prompting to infer initial-state predicates, and GPT-4 to compose goal specifications from the instruction and detected state; all assembled into executable PDs without task-specific training.
paper_title: Vision-Language Interpreter for Robot Task Planning

problem: Automatically generated PDDL problems often contain syntactic/semantic inconsistencies that prevent planners from finding valid plans.
method: Corrective Re-prompting with Chain-of-Thought: put the symbolic planner in the loop and feed its error messages to GPT-4, which first explains the error (CoT) and then revises the PD iteratively until errors disappear, yielding >99% syntactic correctness and higher plan validity.
paper_title: Vision-Language Interpreter for Robot Task Planning

problem: There is no standardized dataset or metrics to evaluate multimodal PDDL problem description generation across domains.
method: ProDG dataset and metrics: a three-domain benchmark (Cooking, Blocksworld, Hanoi) with images, instructions, and PDDL domain/problems, plus domain-agnostic metrics (Rsyntax, Rplan, Rpart, Rall) to assess logical validity and semantic coverage of generated PDs.
paper_title: Vision-Language Interpreter for Robot Task Planning

problem: NLP models’ robustness is undermined by spurious correlations, but existing analyses focus on tokens/syntax and lack a way to detect and quantify shortcut use at the broader concept level due to missing concept labels.
method: Use ChatGPT to annotate concept presence in texts and introduce a metric (Bias@C) based on conditional accuracy gaps with/without a concept to quantify concept-level shortcuts; validate with embedding clustering of concept-associated tokens and apply the measurement across fine-tuning and ICL settings.
paper_title: Explore Spurious Correlations at the Concept Level in Language Models for Text Classification

problem: Language models may exploit concept–label imbalances in both training data and in-context exemplars, but the extent and sensitivity are unclear.
method: Construct concept-biased vs concept-balanced datasets and prompts, then evaluate models with Bias@C and robust accuracies (Acc@C/Acc@NoC); show that imbalanced training or biased exemplars systematically increase concept-level shortcutting and harm utility.
paper_title: Explore Spurious Correlations at the Concept Level in Language Models for Text Classification

problem: Mitigating concept-level spurious correlations without sacrificing accuracy is challenging; simple token masking or downsampling can be ineffective or data-destructive.
method: Propose LLM-generated counterfactual upsampling: inject a target concept into minority-label texts while preserving the original label to rebalance concept–label distributions; this reduces Bias@C and maintains or improves accuracy compared to downsampling and token masking.
paper_title: Explore Spurious Correlations at the Concept Level in Language Models for Text Classification

problem: Graph-based RAG methods (community- or ego-network-based) retrieve large, redundant neighborhoods that introduce noise and inflate token usage for global, cross-document questions.
method: Retrieve query-relevant nodes via keyword extraction + dense matching, then select only key relational paths between node pairs using a distance-aware, flow-based pruning algorithm that assigns reliability scores and keeps top-K paths, cutting redundancy and token cost while preserving salient relations.
paper_title: PathRAG: Pruning Graph-Based Retrieval Augmented Generation with Relational Paths

problem: Flat concatenation of retrieved nodes/edges in prompts ignores structural relations and suffers from LLM 'lost-in-the-middle', harming logicality and coherence.
method: Path-based prompting: convert each selected path into a textual relational path by concatenating node and edge contexts; order paths by reliability with the most reliable placed at the end and the query at the beginning to leverage primacy/recency effects, improving coherence and reasoning.
paper_title: PathRAG: Pruning Graph-Based Retrieval Augmented Generation with Relational Paths

problem: Existing KG-RAG path selection targets local, single-entity answers and lacks efficient filtering for multiple candidate paths needed by global tasks.
method: A scalable flow-based pruning with decay and early stopping propagates 'resource' over the graph, assigns edge flow scores, and ranks paths by average flow (reliability), yielding low time complexity and robust, token-efficient path selection even under graph sparsity.
paper_title: PathRAG: Pruning Graph-Based Retrieval Augmented Generation with Relational Paths

problem: Deepfake detectors either provide a binary label or a text explanation, and CLIP-based detectors lack prompts that cover diverse/manipulation-specific artifacts, limiting generalization and localization.
method: Forgery Prompt Learning (FPL): learn Universal Forgery Prompts with (i) general forgery tokens capturing cross-method invariants and (ii) image-conditioned specific tokens derived from the CLIP image embedding; insert trainable layer-wise forgery tokens into a frozen CLIP text encoder to adapt it to forensics. Use the resulting text embedding to compute patch-wise text–image similarity, yielding forged attention maps that guide the detector and improve generalization to unseen forgeries.
paper_title: Rethinking Vision-Language Model in Face Forensics: Multi-Modal Interpretable Forged Face Detector

problem: How to leverage the strong open-set features of a frozen CLIP image encoder while injecting deepfake-specific knowledge for robust detection across datasets.
method: Bridge Adapter (Bri-Ada): a transformer-based adapter that ingests intermediate features from the frozen CLIP image encoder and a dedicated deepfake encoder, fuses them, and—guided by the forged attention map—produces a refined forgery vector for classification. This end-to-end scheme reuses CLIP’s representations while adding domain-specific cues, trained with only binary labels.
paper_title: Rethinking Vision-Language Model in Face Forensics: Multi-Modal Interpretable Forged Face Detector

problem: Existing VLMs/LLMs provide weak or unstable deepfake rationales because they are not aligned with forensics-specific cues (e.g., frequency artifacts) or the detector’s evidence.
method: Forgery Explanation Module: transform the fused detection representation into a frequency-based token (H_F) and align it—with visual tokens from CLIP—into a frozen LLM via lightweight projectors and LoRA fine-tuning. Concatenating H_F with visual tokens conditions the LLM to generate trustworthy, detection-consistent judgments and explanations grounded in deepfake-specific (frequency and spatial) cues.
paper_title: Rethinking Vision-Language Model in Face Forensics: Multi-Modal Interpretable Forged Face Detector

problem: Instruction-tuned LLM distillation is typically one-way imitation and cannot target student weaknesses; classical adversarial KD requires teacher parameters/logits, making it inapplicable to proprietary black-box LLMs.
method: An adversarial, feedback-driven distillation loop that leverages the proprietary LLM via prompting in three roles: (1) Teacher to provide reference responses, (2) Referee to score teacher vs. student outputs and identify ‘hard’ instructions with large quality gaps, and (3) Generator to synthesize new hard instructions in the same domain/task type. Iterative imitation–discrimination–generation focuses training on hard samples, enabling black-box AKD without access to teacher weights or logits.
paper_title: Lion: Adversarial Distillation of Proprietary Large Language Models

problem: Hard-sample focus can cause data imbalance and catastrophic forgetting, and large-scale human-labeled data is costly; need data-efficient, diverse instruction generation under a tight budget.
method: A data-efficient curation strategy: maintain Train and Cache pools; use bias-mitigated LLM-as-judge scoring with a threshold to label hard/easy; prompt-generate both hard variants and long-tail easy instructions at a 1:1 ratio; enforce ROUGE-L de-dup to ensure novelty. With ~70k synthetic pairs (no human annotation), the student (Lion-13B) approaches ChatGPT on open-ended generation and substantially surpasses Vicuna-13B on BBH (+55.4% avg) and AGIEval (+16.7% avg).
paper_title: Lion: Adversarial Distillation of Proprietary Large Language Models

problem: Existing evaluations overestimate LLMs’ ability to track discourse entities due to shortcuts (e.g., emptying operations, slot-filling, lexical cues) and lack of form–meaning disentanglement.
method: Design a synthetic, controllable entity-tracking benchmark with natural-language descriptions of boxes and objects followed by sequences of operations; query each box after each step via cloze; enforce anti-shortcut splits (signature-based deduping across splits), low lexical overlap variants (AltForms), and context-dependent operations (MoveContents, ambiguous references) to require sequential composition.
paper_title: Entity Tracking in Language Models

problem: Do pretrained LLMs exhibit non-trivial entity tracking, and which training factors make this capacity surface?
method: Systematically evaluate GPT-3, GPT-3.5, and Flan-T5 with in-context demonstrations on base and harder variants; compare models with/without code pretraining and RLHF. Finding: only GPT-3.5/code-trained models outperform a strong random baseline and maintain accuracy across multiple operations; performance persists under AltForms, implicating code pretraining (rather than size or instruction tuning alone) as key.
paper_title: Entity Tracking in Language Models

problem: Can smaller text-pretrained models learn entity tracking that generalizes beyond the training distribution, and how crucial is pretraining?
method: Finetune T5-base on the benchmark under diverse train/test mismatches (Base, NumOps, Vocab, AltForms, AmbiRef, MoveContents). Results: near-perfect accuracy on Base; nontrivial generalization to longer sequences and unseen vocab; substantial degradation under strong form shifts and context-dependent ops; pretraining is necessary (randomly initialized T5 collapses to empty predictions).
paper_title: Entity Tracking in Language Models

problem: Few/zero-shot node classification on text-attributed graphs lacks supervision, and per-node LLM augmentation is costly and ineffective with scarce labels.
method: LLM4NG performs class-level augmentation by prompting an LLM with label semantics to generate a small set of labeled synthetic node texts per class, then encodes both real and synthetic nodes with lightweight sentence embeddings (e.g., Sentence-BERT/e5) to inject supervision signals; claimed as the first use of LLMs for node generation on graphs.
paper_title: Leveraging Large Language Models for Node Generation in Few-Shot Learning on Text-Attributed Graphs

problem: Generated nodes must be integrated into the graph to propagate supervision, but naive similarity-based linking is noisy and unreliable.
method: A two-stage edge integration: (1) cosine-similarity prefiltering to create candidate links between synthetic and real nodes; (2) a supervised edge predictor (MLP on concatenated node embeddings) trained on raw graph positive/negative edges to score candidates, after which top-k edges are added to the graph to enable effective message passing.
paper_title: Leveraging Large Language Models for Node Generation in Few-Shot Learning on Text-Attributed Graphs

problem: Existing LLM-enhanced graph methods require many API calls and heavy computation, limiting scalability on large graphs.
method: A plug-and-play, low-cost pipeline that calls the LLM only M×|C| times (per class, not per node), uses frozen sentence encoders, and trains any GNN on the augmented graph, enabling strong zero-/few-shot performance (e.g., 76% relative gain in 1-shot ogbn-arxiv) with orders-of-magnitude fewer LLM calls.
paper_title: Leveraging Large Language Models for Node Generation in Few-Shot Learning on Text-Attributed Graphs

problem: Retrieving the correct multi-hop subgraph from a large KG for KGQA is unstable and costly, often requiring many LLM calls and exploring a vast search space.
method: Relation-centric, hop-aware retrieval: fine-tune a PLM (e.g., BERT) to predict question hop count and extract anchor entities; run constrained BFS using relations as retrieval units; have the LLM score relation links for semantic relevance; sample top-k paths to build a compact reasoning graph.
paper_title: LightPROF: A Lightweight Reasoning Framework for Large Language Model on Knowledge Graph

problem: Text-only KG prompts lose critical structural information and bloat context windows, hindering LLM reasoning over complex KG structures.
method: A Transformer-based Knowledge Adapter that fuses fine-grained textual and structural signals per reasoning path, encoding each path into a single token; a projector maps these fused vectors into the LLM’s token embedding space to form ‘knowledge soft prompts,’ the first to inject both KG text and structure as embeddings for prompting.
paper_title: LightPROF: A Lightweight Reasoning Framework for Large Language Model on Knowledge Graph

problem: Adapting LLMs to KGQA typically needs expensive fine-tuning and large models; small LLMs underperform without efficient knowledge injection.
method: Freeze the LLM and mix learned soft prompts (from the Knowledge Adapter) with carefully designed hard prompts; train only the adapter and projector via next-token prediction. This plug-and-play setup works with various open-source LLMs, achieving parameter-efficient, faster reasoning with far fewer input tokens.
paper_title: LightPROF: A Lightweight Reasoning Framework for Large Language Model on Knowledge Graph

problem: No established task or large-scale data to study live video commenting that depends on both dynamic visual content and surrounding viewer comments.
method: Formulate automatic live commenting as conditioning on a timestamped window of nearby video frames and neighboring comments; release a large-scale Bilibili dataset (2,361 videos, 895,929 timestamped comments) covering 19 categories to enable data-driven modeling.
paper_title: LiveBot: Generating Live Video Comments Based on Visual and Textual Contexts

problem: Modeling complex dependencies between video clips and surrounding comments to generate contextually appropriate live comments.
method: Propose two joint vision–language generators: (1) Fusional RNN with CNN+LSTM video encoder and hierarchical LSTM text encoder with attention that injects video context into sentence representations, and a decoder attending to both modalities; (2) Unified Transformer that concatenates neighboring comments into a single sequence and uses multi-head self- and cross-attention to video features, with a decoder attending to both video and text. Both outperform seq2seq baselines.
paper_title: LiveBot: Generating Live Video Comments Based on Visual and Textual Contexts

problem: Standard reference-based generation metrics (e.g., BLEU/ROUGE) are unreliable for open-ended live comments.
method: Introduce a retrieval-based evaluation protocol: rank a candidate set (Correct/Plausible/Popular/Random) by log-likelihood and assess with Recall@k, Mean Rank, and MRR, providing a robust, discriminative evaluation for live commenting.
paper_title: LiveBot: Generating Live Video Comments Based on Visual and Textual Contexts

problem: Vision-and-Language Pre-training (VLP) relies on heavy CNN-based region/grid visual embedders that dominate compute, slow inference, and cap expressiveness via predefined visual vocabularies.
method: ViLT replaces CNNs and region supervision with a simple linear patch projection (32×32) for images and a single-stream transformer (initialized from ViT) that jointly processes image patches and text tokens; this monolithic, convolution-free design shifts compute to multimodal interaction and yields large speedups (tens× over region features, ≥4× over grid features) with competitive accuracy.
paper_title: ViLT: Vision-and-Language Transformer Without Convolution or Region Supervision

problem: Standard VLP training underuses augmentation and subword-level masking lets MLM be solved from text context alone, reducing cross-modal learning.
method: Adopt whole word masking during pre-training to force reliance on visual context, and apply RandAugment during fine-tuning (enabled by on-the-fly patch embeddings instead of cached features); both yield consistent gains on VQAv2, NLVR2, and retrieval.
paper_title: ViLT: Vision-and-Language Transformer Without Convolution or Region Supervision

problem: Without region supervision, models lack an explicit mechanism to align words with corresponding image parts at fine granularity.
method: Introduce Word-Patch Alignment (WPA): compute an optimal-transport-based alignment (IPOT) between textual tokens and visual patches and add the approximate Wasserstein distance to the ITM loss, encouraging fine-grained cross-modal alignment.
paper_title: ViLT: Vision-and-Language Transformer Without Convolution or Region Supervision

problem: Small graph datasets make it hard for GNNs to learn invariant, causal subgraph rationales; prior pooling or distribution-intervention methods (e.g., DIR) struggle to identify optimal rationales and generalize.
method: Introduce environment-based augmentations that treat non-causal environments as noise: (1) environment removal (train on rationale only) and (2) environment replacement (combine a graph’s rationale with other graphs’ environments) while enforcing the original label. This creates virtual samples that encourage invariant rationales and robustness.
paper_title: Graph Rationalization with Environment-based Augmentations

problem: Explicit decoding/encoding of subgraphs and cross-graph merging for augmentation is computationally expensive and technically difficult.
method: Perform rationalization and augmentation purely in latent space (GREA): learn node-level rationale probabilities via GNN1+MLP1; get contextual node embeddings via GNN2; derive rationale, environment, and replaced-example representations through masked pooling and simple aggregation (e.g., sum), trained with removal/replacement losses and rationale-size regularization in an alternating separator/predictor scheme.
paper_title: Graph Rationalization with Environment-based Augmentations

problem: Edge-selection based rationales can produce incoherent subgraphs and poor interpretability/prediction on real molecule/polymer data.
method: Use node-mask based rationale separation and latent aggregation to yield coherent node-set rationales aligned with domain knowledge, improving accuracy and interpretability across multiple molecular and polymer property benchmarks.
paper_title: Graph Rationalization with Environment-based Augmentations

problem: Pre-trained vision-language models (e.g., UNITER) are large and costly; it is unclear whether sparse subnetworks can retain performance across diverse VL tasks.
method: Apply Iterative Magnitude-based Pruning (IMP) with reset to pre-trained weights (not random init) on UNITER across 7 VL tasks; evaluate rewinding and alternative initializations. Discover 'relaxed' winning tickets at 50–70% sparsity that maintain ~99% of full accuracy, and show the learned mask and pre-trained initialization are crucial (random pruning/shuffled init degrade).
paper_title: Playing Lottery Tickets with Vision and Language

problem: Finding task-specific tickets via IMP is expensive; can one task-agnostic subnetwork transfer well to many VL tasks?
method: Run IMP during pre-training (on MLM/MRM/ITM/WRA with COCO/VG/CC/SBU) to learn a universal mask, then finetune on all downstream tasks. The task-agnostic subnetwork at 60%/70% sparsity matches ~98%/96% of full accuracy on average and transfers competitively versus task-specific tickets; downstream-to-downstream transfer is also strong.
paper_title: Playing Lottery Tickets with Vision and Language

problem: Do lottery-ticket behaviors persist across different VLP architectures and under adversarial training?
method: Evaluate IMP on LXMERT (two-stream) and ViLT (end-to-end patches). LXMERT mirrors UNITER (winning tickets up to ~70% sparsity), while ViLT tolerates much less pruning (~30%). Incorporate adversarial training (feature-space PGD with KL regularization) to find/enhance tickets, achieving comparable accuracy at ~10% higher sparsity and consistent gains at 60–70% sparsity.
paper_title: Playing Lottery Tickets with Vision and Language

problem: Grounded vision-language tasks (e.g., grounded captioning) require generating text, object boxes, and their word–box alignments; prior systems use separate detectors/heads and post-hoc alignment scores, increasing complexity and limiting generalization.
method: Model both text and boxes as a single auto-regressive sequence from one shared encoder–decoder. Quantize box coordinates into discrete tokens (à la Pix2Seq) and unify text+box vocabularies so the decoder can code-switch between them. Introduce a special <obj> token before grounded words and after box tokens to naturally encode word–box alignments inline, eliminating separate alignment modules and any explicit detector.
paper_title: UniTAB: Unifying Text and Box Outputs for Grounded Vision-Language Modeling

problem: Building a parameter-efficient, general-purpose VL model that handles diverse output formats (text, boxes, or both) and generalizes to new tasks typically requires task-specific heads and models.
method: Use a task-agnostic text+box output sequence and a single language-modeling objective for pre-training, multi-task finetuning, and optional task-specific finetuning. Train one model jointly on 7 VL benchmarks (captioning, VQA, visual grounding, grounded captioning) without task-specific heads, enabling parameter sharing and zero-shot transfer to new tasks (e.g., ImageNet object localization) by reformatting outputs into the unified sequence.
paper_title: UniTAB: Unifying Text and Box Outputs for Grounded Vision-Language Modeling

problem: Efficiently adapting large vision-language models to few-shot downstream tasks without forgetting pre-trained knowledge or over-relying on it; prompt tuning discards the pre-trained classifier while adapter-style tuning is constrained by pre-trained features.
method: Task Residual Tuning (TaskRes): freeze the VLM’s text-based classifier (pre-trained text embeddings) and learn a prior-independent residual matrix per class (scaled by α, optionally learnable) that is additively applied to the classifier weights. Only the residual is trained, decoupling prior knowledge from task-specific knowledge, requiring minimal parameters and no repeated text-encoder runs.
paper_title: Task Residual for Tuning Vision-Language Models

problem: Determining how much adaptation is needed per task and ensuring the pre-trained decision boundary is preserved during tuning.
method: Introduce a relative transfer difficulty metric (random-baseline precision vs zero-shot precision) and empirically show the learned residual magnitude scales with task difficulty; analyze Wrong2Right/Right2Wrong counts to demonstrate TaskRes better preserves the pre-trained boundary while correcting errors.
paper_title: Task Residual for Tuning Vision-Language Models

problem: Strengthening the base classifier for downstream adaptation without sacrificing the decoupled, parameter-efficient tuning.
method: Enhanced base classifier: briefly fine-tune only the text projection layer to improve the base text embeddings, then apply TaskRes on top. This maintains the residual decoupling and yields higher source accuracy with minimal additional complexity.
paper_title: Task Residual for Tuning Vision-Language Models

problem: Parameter-efficient fine-tuning across many NLP and vision-and-language tasks typically requires separate task-specific modules (adapters/prefixes) per layer/block, inflating parameters and limiting cross-task transfer.
method: A shared hypernetwork consumes compositional hyper-embeddings (task, layer, block, and optional visual embeddings) to generate on-the-fly weights for small modules in a frozen T5 (prefix vectors for attention, adapter weights for FFN). Only the hypernetwork, embeddings, and layer norms are trained, unifying tuning across tasks/blocks with strong multi-task sharing and far fewer parameters.
paper_title: HyperPELT: Unified Parameter-Efficient Language Model Tuning for Both Language and Vision-and-Language Tasks

problem: Standard prefix-tuning uses per-layer embeddings and MLPs, leading to large per-task parameter counts and training overhead.
method: HyperPrefix: extend hyper-embeddings to the prefix length and project them with a lightweight hypernetwork to directly produce key/value prefixes conditioned on task, layer, and block. This replaces per-layer prefix parameters with a compact generator, cutting parameters by an order of magnitude while matching or improving performance.
paper_title: HyperPELT: Unified Parameter-Efficient Language Model Tuning for Both Language and Vision-and-Language Tasks

problem: Adapting text-pretrained LMs to V&L tasks efficiently usually appends image tokens to inputs, consuming context length and requiring many trainable parameters.
method: VL-HyperPELT: derive visual hyper-embeddings from CLIP image features via a visual projector, then use visual hypernetworks to generate visual-specific attention prefixes and adapter weights. Visual and task prefixes are concatenated and adapters inserted in parallel, enabling multimodal capability without extending the input sequence and with minimal trainable parameters, improving V&L multi-task and few-shot transfer.
paper_title: HyperPELT: Unified Parameter-Efficient Language Model Tuning for Both Language and Vision-and-Language Tasks

problem: Existing bounds relating contrastive (InfoNCE) loss to downstream classification loss are either valid only for limited negative sample sizes K (often K≥C) or become vacuous/explode with K due to coverage/collision terms, leaving no clear theory for how K affects downstream performance.
method: Derive tight surrogate bounds—both upper and lower—on the mean supervised loss as linear functions of the contrastive loss with a constant coefficient independent of K and C, valid for all K; proofs leverage the shared log-sum-exp structure and bounded embeddings via Jensen’s inequality, establishing an O(1/K) decay of the surrogate gap so the contrastive loss is a sound surrogate objective.
paper_title: On the Surrogate Gap between Contrastive and Supervised Losses

problem: Uncertainty whether larger K changes the attainable downstream optimum or only estimation quality, with prior claims positing a collision–coverage trade-off.
method: Introduce a feasible-region analysis in the (R_cont, R_μ-supv) plane using essential bounds to show the optimal downstream loss remains attainable for any K, while the upper–lower surrogate gap shrinks as O(1/K); this removes the collision–coverage trade-off and explains monotonic gains from increasing K.
paper_title: On the Surrogate Gap between Contrastive and Supervised Losses

problem: Lack of empirical confirmation that a K-agnostic surrogate theory matches practice across modalities and datasets.
method: Validate on synthetic data, CIFAR-10/100, and Wiki-3029 by plugging measured contrastive losses into the new bounds and comparing to true supervised losses and learning trajectories; show the proposed bounds are tightest among prior work, valid for all K, and capture dynamics (slope and variance reduction with larger K).
paper_title: On the Surrogate Gap between Contrastive and Supervised Losses

problem: Existing factuality metrics for abstractive summarization ignore the vision modality, making them inadequate for multimodal (image+text) summarization where summaries must be faithful to both document and images.
method: CLIPBERTSCORE: a simple weighted combination of CLIPScore (image–summary cosine similarity via CLIP, averaged over multi-image/multi-sentence pairs) and BERTScore precision (document–summary entailment-style similarity using RoBERTa-large-MNLI), with a tunable weight α. Despite being zero-shot, it achieves strong, robust correlations with human judgments and competes with fine-tuned multimodal metrics.
paper_title: Evaluating and Improving Factuality in Multimodal Abstractive Summarization

problem: There is no meta-evaluation benchmark with human annotations to assess multimodal factuality metrics.
method: MUFAME: a meta-evaluation benchmark on WikiHow with human judgments for document-only, image-only, and combined factuality; plus WikiHowFact, a ranking task to test modality-aware factuality. Using these, CLIPBERTSCORE outperforms text-only and multimodal baselines and is robust across FOIL, BISON, and FRANK evaluations.
paper_title: Evaluating and Improving Factuality in Multimodal Abstractive Summarization

problem: Multimodal summarization models hallucinate and lack mechanisms to leverage images to improve factuality during training.
method: Use CLIPBERTSCORE for training-time interventions: (1) select salient images for visual guidance, and (2) serve as a reward in self-critical sequence training (alternated with ROUGE-2). Both strategies improve automatic factuality metrics and human-judged faithfulness on MMSS and MSMO.
paper_title: Evaluating and Improving Factuality in Multimodal Abstractive Summarization

problem: Accuracy- and ablation-based tests cannot reliably measure how much each modality contributes to a VL model’s prediction (and are ill-defined for ISA), making unimodal collapse hard to detect even when predictions are wrong or tasks are low-accuracy/zero-shot.
method: Introduce MM-SHAP, a performance-agnostic multimodality score built on Shapley values. Compute token-level contributions by masking words ([MASK]) and image patches (zeroed pixels), approximate with Monte Carlo coalitions, then aggregate absolute Shapley values per modality to get T-SHAP/V-SHAP proportions. Leverages Shapley efficiency/additivity to support sample-, dataset-, and model-level analyses and extends to any number of modalities.
paper_title: MM-SHAP: A Performance-agnostic Metric for Measuring Multimodal Contributions in Vision and Language Models & Tasks

problem: Lack of a principled tool to compare and diagnose modality reliance across models, datasets, tasks, and under fine-tuning, including when standard metrics are unreliable.
method: Apply MM-SHAP to six VL models (LXMERT, CLIP, four ALBEF variants) over four tasks (ISA, VQA, GQA, VALSE). Use additivity to average per-sample scores for dataset/model comparisons, revealing modality biases and their directions (e.g., CLIP balanced, LXMERT more visual, ALBEF more textual) and showing that fine-tuning shifts multimodal degree and can degrade ISA despite high task performance elsewhere.
paper_title: MM-SHAP: A Performance-agnostic Metric for Measuring Multimodal Contributions in Vision and Language Models & Tasks

problem: Existing explanations (e.g., attention) struggle to attribute fine-grained, cross-modal evidence—including negative evidence—hindering error analysis and bias diagnosis.
method: Use token-level Shapley attributions within MM-SHAP to visualize positive/negative contributions of words and image patches per instance, enabling detection of ‘right answer for the wrong reasons,’ stability of modality use vs. accuracy, and exposure of dataset/model biases (e.g., counting, existence, spatial relations) to guide debugging and data cleaning.
paper_title: MM-SHAP: A Performance-agnostic Metric for Measuring Multimodal Contributions in Vision and Language Models & Tasks

problem: Existing LVLM benchmarks cover few tasks and rudimentary skills, failing to measure breadth needed for multitask AGI across diverse modalities and expert domains.
method: Introduce MMT-Bench: 31,325 multi-choice visual questions spanning 32 meta-tasks and 162 subtasks over 13 visual types (natural, charts, medical, depth, screenshots, videos, point clouds, etc.). Build a hierarchical task structure and a unified metadata-driven pipeline that repurposes public datasets, generates options/answers via rules or ChatGPT, and standardizes evaluation with robust answer extraction—enabling wide, expert-level assessment of recognition, localization, reasoning, OCR, 3D, temporal, planning, and more.
paper_title: MMT-Bench: A Comprehensive Multimodal Benchmark for Evaluating Large Vision-Language Models Towards Multitask AGI

problem: Developers lack a principled way to relate multimodal tasks and detect in-/out-of-domain weaknesses or predict transfer/generalization across tasks.
method: Construct a task map by deriving task vectors per subtask via LoRA fine-tuning of QwenVL-Chat and computing cosine distances; analyze Kendall’s tau ranking correlations and perform hierarchical clustering to identify in-domain (e.g., recognition/captioning) and out-of-domain (e.g., localization, fine-grained pixel perception, GUI/structured data) clusters, providing actionable guidance for model development and evaluation.
paper_title: MMT-Bench: A Comprehensive Multimodal Benchmark for Evaluating Large Vision-Language Models Towards Multitask AGI

problem: There is limited understanding of how LVLMs behave under different prompting/settings and what error modes dominate across many tasks.
method: Evaluate 30 LVLMs (open/closed) on MMT-Bench under multiple input regimes (single vs multi-image, visual referring prompts, coordinate formats) and perform expert-labeled error taxonomy. Findings include: multi-image prompting boosts performance (even for single-image LVLMs via embedding concatenation), visual referring prompting lags due to lack of SFT data, instruction tuning can hurt some closed-set tasks, and dominant failure modes are perception and reasoning errors.
paper_title: MMT-Bench: A Comprehensive Multimodal Benchmark for Evaluating Large Vision-Language Models Towards Multitask AGI

problem: Selective prediction for vision-language reasoning abstains excessively at low risk tolerances, discarding many correct answers and yielding impractically low coverage.
method: ReCoVERR, a training-free inference-time procedure that, upon a low-confidence VLM answer, converts it to a hypothesis and iteratively collects visual evidence by (i) LLM-generated, targeted sub-questions answered by the VLM (and vision tools) and kept only if reliable (VLM confidence ≥ 1−r) and relevant (defeasible relevance via NLI: change in entailment when flipping evidence vs its negation ≥ δmin), and (ii) decides to answer if aggregated evidence entails the hypothesis with NLI probability ≥ πNLImin; otherwise abstains—thus raising coverage without exceeding the risk budget.
paper_title: Selective 'Selective Prediction': Reducing Unnecessary Abstention in Vision-Language Reasoning

problem: Generative VLMs lack well-calibrated confidence estimates needed to gate evidence reliability and selective prediction decisions.
method: Self-Prompting confidence estimation: after generating an answer, prompt the VLM to verify it with a yes/no query and use the yes-vs-no next-token probabilities (optionally Platt-scaled) as a calibrated confidence score, outperforming token-probability baselines and enabling reliable evidence filtering required by ReCoVERR.
paper_title: Selective 'Selective Prediction': Reducing Unnecessary Abstention in Vision-Language Reasoning

problem: VLMs hallucinate objects because multi-scale visual features are integrated indiscriminately, diluting object-specific evidence with background noise.
method: A training-free selective multi-scale integration (SECOND) that adds low-/mid-resolution vision encoders via positional-embedding interpolation and decodes in coarse-to-fine stages. At each stage, it computes a visual attention map (vision self-attn × language cross-attn) and dynamically selects only top-attended patches for the next higher-resolution stage using entropy-controlled p_select, accumulating object-relevant patches and improving grounding.
paper_title: SECOND: Mitigating Perceptual Hallucination in Vision-Language Models via Selective and Contrastive Decoding

problem: Even with better visual selection, generations can still follow language priors; we need to bias decoding toward evidence from fine-grained, relevant regions.
method: Multi-stage Contrastive Decoding that treats early-stage (sparse) outputs as amateurs and the final (dense, selected) output as the expert. It contrasts logits across successive stages with weights (α, β, γ), leveraging sparse-vs-dense visual differences rather than only a single amateur-expert pair, yielding stronger visual grounding and reduced hallucinations.
paper_title: SECOND: Mitigating Perceptual Hallucination in Vision-Language Models via Selective and Contrastive Decoding

problem: Lack of a principled link between visual attention focus and hallucination hinders targeted mitigation.
method: Introduce hallucination probability P_Hal and an Attention Dice Coefficient combining vision self-attention and text cross-attention aligned with object masks. Prove that (i) smaller patches increase Attention Dice when attention aligns (Thm 3.2) and (ii) selective multi-stage patch selection monotonically improves/lower-bounds Attention Dice across stages (Thm 3.3), motivating selective multi-scale decoding.
paper_title: SECOND: Mitigating Perceptual Hallucination in Vision-Language Models via Selective and Contrastive Decoding

problem: Existing universal adversarial perturbations (UAPs) for CLIP/VLMs fail to produce a single perturbation that reliably transfers across data, domains, models, and tasks (super transferability), and often assume fixed, costly surrogate ensembles.
method: X-Transfer generates non-targeted and targeted UAPs directly in CLIP embedding space with a generic, encoder-agnostic loss (averaging losses, not embeddings) over diverse surrogate CLIP encoders. This produces one perturbation that achieves simultaneous cross-data, cross-domain, cross-model, and cross-task transferability to both CLIP encoders and downstream VLMs under strict black-box settings.
paper_title: X-Transfer Attacks: Towards Super Transferable Adversarial Attacks on CLIP

problem: Scaling transfer attacks with many surrogate models is computationally prohibitive and naive fixed-ensemble choices underperform when the surrogate pool is large and heterogeneous.
method: Surrogate scaling via dynamic selection: treat each surrogate encoder as an arm in a non-stationary multi-armed bandit and select the top-k per step using UCB scores, with the loss as reward to prioritize hard-to-fool encoders. This reduces compute to roughly k/N while improving transfer, and can work with as few as one surrogate per step, yielding state-of-the-art black-box super transferability.
paper_title: X-Transfer Attacks: Towards Super Transferable Adversarial Attacks on CLIP
